cit.q: kaḻal kaṉi vakutta tuṇaic cil lōtik | kuṟum toṭi makaḷir
kaḻal malku pantoṭu ammāṉai muṟṟil kaṟṟavar ciṟṟu iṭaik kaṉṉimārkaḷ
young unmarried girls who have a small waist and have learnt the games of playing with molucca beans, large number of balls, ammāṉai, and small winnowsTranslated by .

sense.note: Here the for line 23 with an unusually detailed description of the women’s hair-do reads: kaḻaṉmaṇiyiṉatu kaṉiyāl kūṟupaṭuttuc curuṭṭappaṭṭa iṉamākiya cilavākiya paṉiccai, presumably “a Paṉiccai-mode in which there are few that are groups [of hair] which have been rolled in portions by means of the ripe fruit of the Kaḻaṉmaṇi”. Here one would expect Kaḻaṉmaṇi to be a proper name for a plant, but this is not attested or discussed elsewhere. Instead, for kaḻal a sub-entry with reference to this passage is found in the TL, which equates kaḻal here with kaḻaṟci, “molucca bean”. This does not appear justified either by the text of the PN nor by its commentary. Of course we may speculate that women used the molucca pods as curlers, but one would want parallels. Perhaps another trace of the general idea is found in 1.4.2.1 where kaḻal is enumerated under a series of girl’s games and translated by as moluca beans (a game otherwise known under the name of kaḻaṅku.

It must be noted, however, that the Nikaṇṭu-s support the idea. Not in the critical edition of the , but in (currently) two palm-leaf manuscripts (from the UVSL and from the TU) have kotikkaḻal as a head word for the entry on molluca beans: kaḻaṅku-ē mūḻal koṭikkaḻal eṉak karutuvar, “they consider mūḻal and koṭikkaḻal as molucca beans (kaḻaṅku).” The current edition of the (2885) reads the virtually identical sutra with kaḻaṟkoṭi as its headword.

cit.q: a little village … with a thorn fence of molucca plants

bibl.title: A comprehensive Tamil and English dictionary (Winslow)

cit.q: kaḻaṉmaṇiyiṉatu kaṉiyāl kūṟupaṭuttuc curuṭṭappaṭṭa iṉamākiya cilavākiya paṉiccai
a Paṉiccai-mode in which there are few that are groups [of hair] which have been rolled in portions by means of the ripe fruit of the Kaḻaṉmaṇi
